tracks.title: Junior ML Engineer
tracks.description: Screening and the first technical round: ML fundamentals, metrics, validation, sklearn. 12 questions that come up in almost every interview.
tracks.advice: A junior interview tests your fundamentals and your ability to reason out loud. Don't go silent: narrate your thinking, honestly say 'I don't know, but here's how I'd reason'. Memorized definitions without understanding are spotted instantly.

tracks.title: Middle ML Engineer
tracks.description: Production experience: leaks, imbalance, A/B tests, drift, deployment. 12 questions separating 'took a course' from 'worked with live systems'.
tracks.advice: A middle is distinguished by production scars. Try to answer every question with the scheme: a short direct answer → an example from practice (or from this simulator) → the trade-offs. The interviewer listens to how you think about the consequences of decisions.

tracks.title: ML System Design
tracks.description: Design a whole system: 8 tasks in real design-interview format with model walkthroughs following the 7-step framework.
tracks.advice: The main design-interview mistake is drawing the architecture right away. The first 5 minutes are questions only: business goal, scale, latency, data. Walk the interviewer through the framework explicitly: 'first I'll clarify requirements, then metrics, then data...' — the structure of your answer is graded above the details.

junior.q: How does supervised learning differ from unsupervised? Give two business examples of each.
junior.a:
Supervised — learning from labeled 'input → correct answer' pairs: the model learns to reproduce the labels. Examples: churn prediction (classification), delivery cost prediction (regression). Unsupervised — no labels; we look for structure: customer segmentation via clustering, anomaly detection in transactions.

A strong answer adds: in real business ~90% of tasks are supervised because their quality is measurable; unsupervised is harder to evaluate — there are no 'correct' clusters, and quality is measured by business usefulness.

junior.q: What is overfitting and how do you fight it?
junior.a:
Overfitting is the model learning the training set's noise instead of the pattern: great metrics on train, a flop on new data. Diagnosis: a large gap between train and validation metrics.

Remedies: (1) more data; (2) a simpler model (shallower trees, fewer parameters); (3) regularization — L1/L2, dropout for networks; (4) early stopping on validation; (5) for networks — data augmentation.

Interview bonus: name the mirror problem — underfitting (both metrics bad) — and that the diagnosis always comes from the PAIR of train/val curves, not from one.

junior.q: Why can't you evaluate a model on the same data it was trained on?
junior.a:
Because you'd measure memory, not generalization. The model could simply memorize the training examples (a deep tree or kNN with k=1 gives near-100% on train). ML's job is to work on unseen data, so quality is measured on a held-out test.

The fuller answer mentions the three-way split: train (fitting) / validation (hyperparameter tuning, early stopping) / test (the final estimate, used once). If the test participated in any decision — it's 'spoiled' and inflates the estimate.

junior.q: The model's accuracy is 99%. Is that a good model?
junior.a:
It depends on the class balance and the cost of errors — the key phrase the interviewer wants. If the positive class occurs in 1% of cases (fraud, defects), the constant 'always negative' also gives 99% accuracy while catching zero positives.

The right approach: compare against a constant baseline; under imbalance look at precision, recall, F1, PR-AUC; choose the metric from error costs (what's costlier — missing fraud or blocking an honest user?). An excellent answer sketches the confusion matrix and explains FP vs FN with an example.

junior.q: Explain the difference between precision and recall. When does each matter more?
junior.a:
Precision = of everyone the model called positive, how many really are (alert purity). Recall = of all actual positives, how many the model found (catch completeness). They trade off through the threshold: lower it → recall grows, precision falls.

When which matters: a spam filter — precision (losing an important email to spam is scary); cancer screening — recall (missing the disease is unacceptable; a false alarm is resolved by a follow-up exam); anti-fraud — a balance driven by economics: the cost of a miss vs the cost of a false block.

junior.q: What does train_test_split(..., stratify=y) do and why?
junior.a:
Stratification keeps the class proportions in train and test the same as in the full sample. Without it, with a rare class (say 5% positives) a random split can put too few (or too many) positives in the test — the metrics become noisy and irreproducible.

Bonus: mention random_state for reproducibility, and that for temporal data a random split is wrong altogether — you need a time split (train on the past, test on the future).

junior.q: What's the idea of gradient descent? What happens if the learning rate is too large or too small?
junior.a:
Gradient descent is iterative loss minimization: compute the gradient (the direction of steepest error increase) and step the opposite way: w = w − α·∇L. Almost everything trains on this principle — from linear regression to neural networks.

Too large a learning rate — the steps overshoot the minimum: the loss oscillates or diverges. Too small — convergence is unbearably slow and you can get stuck. LR is the most important hyperparameter; a practical trick: try 1e-2/1e-3/1e-4 for a few epochs and read the loss curve's shape.

junior.q: Why scale features? Which models need it and which don't?
junior.a:
Features on different scales (age 18–80 and income 20,000–2,000,000) break models that work with distances and gradients: gradient descent converges poorly, kNN/K-Means effectively measure distance by the single largest feature, linear coefficients become incomparable, regularization penalizes unfairly.

Need it: linear models, SVM, kNN, K-Means, neural networks. DON'T need it: trees and ensembles (Random Forest, boosting) — they compare values with thresholds; monotone transformations don't matter to them. Important: the scaler is fit on train only (fit on train, transform on test) — otherwise leakage.

junior.q: What is cross-validation and when do you use it instead of a simple train/test split?
junior.a:
K-fold CV: split the data into K parts, train K times on K−1 parts validating on the remaining one; average the metric. You get both a quality estimate AND its spread (std) — not one random number.

When: little data (every point counts), or you need to compare models/hyperparameters reliably. A simple holdout — when data is plentiful and training is expensive. Key variants: StratifiedKFold for imbalance, GroupKFold when one entity (customer) has multiple rows, TimeSeriesSplit for temporal data — random K-fold is forbidden there.

junior.q: How does L1 regularization differ from L2, and why are they needed at all?
junior.a:
Regularization adds a penalty for large weights to the loss, restraining overfitting. L2 (Ridge) — a penalty of squared weights: shrinks all weights evenly, never to exactly zero. L1 (Lasso) — a penalty of absolute values: zeroes out weak weights exactly, acting as built-in feature selection.

Practice: L2 as the stability default; L1 when there are many features and you want sparsity/interpretability; ElasticNet combines them. In sklearn LogisticRegression the C parameter is the INVERSE regularization strength (smaller C = stronger penalty) — a favorite interview catch.

junior.q: Why is gradient boosting the usual pick for tabular data rather than neural networks?
junior.a:
Tabular data is heterogeneous (money, counters, categories, flags) and its dependencies are piecewise-sharp: 'delinquency > 30 days → risk jumps'. Trees express such thresholds natively in one split; step-like dependencies are hard for networks. Plus boosting needs no scaling, tolerates missing values, trains fast on CPU.

Networks win where features can't be hand-crafted: images, text, audio — there the net learns representations itself. The honest answer: 'on tables I start with LightGBM/CatBoost; on unstructured data with a pre-trained network'.

junior.q: You've been given a new dataset. Describe your first steps before training a model.
junior.a:
(1) EDA: shape, head, info, describe — understand structure and types. (2) Quality: duplicates by the business key, missing values (and WHY they're missing), outliers (investigate, don't blindly delete), date ranges. (3) The target: distribution, class balance, leakage check (features that are consequences of the target). (4) A 5-minute baseline: a constant or simple rule — the reference point. (5) Only then a model.

This question tests professional maturity: the junior who starts with 'I ran XGBoost' loses to the junior who starts with 'I looked at the data'. 60–80% of a real project is data.

middle.q: The model showed 0.97 ROC-AUC offline and flopped in production. Your hypotheses and investigation plan?
middle.a:
A too-good offline metric is itself a clue. Hypotheses by likelihood: (1) target leakage — a feature that's a consequence of the answer (an account status set after the default); (2) time leakage — a random split of temporal data, or features computed using the future; (3) preprocessing leakage — a scaler/encoder fit before the split; (4) duplicates/groups — the same customer in train and test; (5) training/serving skew — production computes features differently.

The plan: check feature importances (one feature > 40% importance — almost surely a leak); re-validate by time; compare offline vs production feature distributions (adversarial validation between train and production logs); inspect the actual inference feature values. Finish with process fixes: a point-in-time check in review, preprocessing only inside a Pipeline.

middle.q: How do you handle 1:1000 class imbalance? Compare the approaches.
middle.a:
Metrics first: accuracy and 'bare' ROC-AUC deceive; look at PR-AUC and recall/precision at the operating point. Approaches: (1) class weights (scale_pos_weight) — the first choice: no data distortion, works with boosting; (2) undersampling — faster training, loses information; (3) oversampling/SMOTE — overfitting risk on duplicates; synthetics are often useless for boosting; (4) threshold tuning — always mandatory, from economics and operational limits.

Two golden rules that test a middle: sampling applies ONLY to train (the test must reflect the real world), and after weights/sampling the probabilities are biased — scoring needs calibration (CalibratedClassifierCV, Platt/isotonic).

middle.q: Design an A/B test for a new recommendation model. Which mistakes most often break such tests?
middle.a:
The design: (1) the decision metric (North star) — revenue/session, fixed BEFORE launch; proxies (CTR) and guardrails (session length, returns, latency) too; (2) the randomization unit — the user; verify no spillovers; (3) sample size and duration from power analysis — the test doesn't stop the moment it 'turns significant'; (4) an A/A test to verify the split.

Common failures: peeking (repeated looks inflate false positives); non-random assignment (by city/platform); optimizing the proxy — CTR grew on clickbait while revenue fell (trust the North star); multiple comparisons without corrections; forgotten guardrails — 'winning' the metric while wrecking latency. Bonus: recommendations have a feedback loop, so keep a slice of traffic on random impressions.

middle.q: What are data drift and concept drift? How do you monitor them and what do you do on detection?
middle.a:
Data drift — the input distribution changed, the relationship held (a new customer segment arrived). Concept drift — the input→output relationship itself changed (after the pandemic 'hasn't logged in for a while' stopped meaning 'churning'). Separately: output drift — a shift in the prediction distribution, the cheapest early signal; and training/serving skew — not drift but a feature-computation bug.

Monitoring: PSI/KS tests on features and scores (PSI > 0.25 — alert); delayed quality as true labels arrive; an 'aging test' — metric decay when retraining is delayed sets the retraining cadence.

The response depends on the type: gradual drift — scheduled retraining; sudden (a release, a process change) — alert, fallback, source investigation; recurring (seasonality) — build it into the model with features or a model ensemble.

middle.q: How do you validate a forecasting model on temporal data? Why doesn't ordinary K-fold work?
middle.a:
K-fold shuffles time: the model trains on the future and 'predicts' the past — the metrics are systematically inflated; production will never look like that. The right way: rolling/expanding windows — train up to T, validate on [T, T+horizon], shift, repeat; the final test is the last untouched period.

The subtleties that mark a middle: aggregate features are also built strictly from the past (shift BEFORE rolling); when forecasting N days ahead production won't have fresh lags — validation must mimic the real horizon (recursive forecasting or direct per-horizon models); GroupKFold logic: one entity must not straddle train/val; a nested scheme if you tune hyperparameters at the same time.

middle.q: Describe a model's path from notebook to production. Which artifacts and processes are mandatory?
middle.a:
(1) Notebook code → a versioned pipeline (git, transformation tests). (2) The model artifact + a dependency lock file + the data version pinned together (MLflow/registry): 'what's in production and what was it trained on' must be answerable in 10 seconds. (3) The service: REST/gRPC with input validation (Pydantic), /health, the model version in every response; Docker. (4) The rollout: shadow mode (predicts in parallel, answers unused) → 5% canary → full rollout; rollback by config, not deployment. (5) Three monitoring layers: service metrics (p99, 5xx), drift (PSI of features and scores), delayed quality. (6) A runbook: the fallback hierarchy (model → previous version → rule), who's on-call.

The key interview line: a model without monitoring and a rollback plan is an incident that hasn't happened yet.

middle.q: What is training/serving skew and how do you prevent it?
middle.a:
It's a divergence between features at training time and at inference: in training 'average basket over 30 days' came from a clean batch warehouse table, in production from a raw stream with different missing-value logic. The model receives the 'wrong' inputs and degrades silently, with no errors in the logs.

Prevention: (1) a feature store or a shared feature library — one code path for training and inference; (2) logging the ACTUAL inference features and training next versions exactly on them; (3) monitoring production vs train feature distributions; (4) an integration test: run the same object through both pipelines and compare the feature vectors bit for bit. One of the most frequent middle+ questions, because skew is the most frequent cause of 'worse in production'.

middle.q: The business asks to 'explain why the model rejected the customer'. Your tools and their limits?
middle.a:
Levels of explainability: (1) global — feature importances (gain/permutation), partial dependence plots (PDP): what drives the model overall; (2) local — SHAP values: each feature's contribution to a specific prediction: 'rejected due to: high payment-to-income ratio (+0.3), two delinquencies (+0.2)'.

The limits worth stating: SHAP explains the model, not reality — these are correlations, not causality; 'feature X raised the risk' doesn't mean 'changing X gets approval'; in regulated domains (lending) one deliberately picks a less accurate but interpretable model (logreg with a scorecard) — the accuracy/explainability trade-off is a business decision, not a technical one.

middle.q: How do you choose a classification threshold for production? Give a framework.
middle.a:
The threshold is a business decision, not the default 0.5. The framework: (1) write down the FP and FN costs in money (missed fraud −$220, a false block −$5); (2) write down the operational constraints (the review team handles 500 alerts/day → the threshold is the score quantile matching that volume); (3) build the precision-recall curve and compute the expected value V(t) = TP(t)·catch_value − FP(t)·false_cost per threshold; (4) take the value maximum within the constraints; (5) monitor: score drift shifts the actual alert volume at a fixed threshold.

Bonus: if probabilities are needed as probabilities (scoring, prioritizing by expected damage P·amount) — calibrate first, threshold second.

middle.q: Your model degraded over six months: the offline metric on fresh data fell from 0.87 to 0.79. How do you investigate?
middle.a:
(1) Segment the degradation: by time (gradual or a jump at a specific date?), by slices (all segments or one region/platform/traffic source?). A dated jump — look for the event: a release, a data-source change, a marketing campaign. (2) Drift analysis: per-feature PSI then-vs-now; adversarial validation of old vs new data — the classifier's top features point at the culprits. (3) Data checks: did a source break (missing values, zeros, unit changes), did the labeling/target definition change. (4) Test the cure: retrain on a fresh window — did quality return? Yes → drift, set up a regular retrain; no → the task itself changed (concept drift), revisit features and framing.

The point to demonstrate: investigation is a hypothesis-driven process, not 'just retrain it'.

middle.q: Feature store: what is it, what problems does it solve, and when do you NOT need one?
middle.a:
A centralized feature repository with two planes: offline (batch tables for training) and online (low-latency access for inference), with consistency guaranteed between them. Solves: training/serving skew (one feature codebase), feature reuse across teams (a catalog), point-in-time correctness (features 'as of the event', no future leaks), pipeline deduplication.

When NOT needed: one model, batch inference, a small team — a shared function library plus discipline suffices. A feature store is an infrastructure investment with upkeep costs; adopting one 'because everyone has one' is the classic example of complexity that doesn't pay for itself. An honest trade-off answer scores higher than vendor marketing.

middle.q: Why is 'optimized the wrong metric' more dangerous than 'undertuned the model'? Give an example.
middle.a:
An undertuned model is a few percent of unclaimed quality; the wrong metric is a system confidently optimizing harm. Examples: recommendations trained on CTR slide into clickbait — clicks grow, revenue and retention fall; anti-fraud optimizing accuracy under imbalance 'optimally' misses all the fraud; demand forecasting with MSE instead of a quantile loss ignores the error-cost asymmetry (out-of-stock costs more than overstock).

The defense: a hierarchy of metrics (loss → offline → online proxy → business metric) with verified links between levels; guardrail metrics in every experiment; the final word belongs to the North star in an A/B, not the offline metric. A middle differs by building the right target first and only then shooting more accurately.

system_design.q: Design a product recommendation feed for a marketplace (2M users, 300K products, 150 ms per response).
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: goal — revenue/session; 3k RPS peak; a 150 ms budget; 50M clickstream events/day.

2. METRICS: North star — revenue/session (A/B). Proxies: CTR, add-to-cart. Offline: recall@100 (retrieval), NDCG@10 (ranking). Guardrails: diversity, return rate, p99 latency.

3. DATA: view/cart/purchase events with weights; the catalog; segments. Cold start: new users → regional bestsellers; new products → content embeddings of descriptions. Feedback loop: 5% of traffic on randomized impressions.

4. MODEL: two stages. Retrieval: two-tower/ALS embeddings + an ANN index (FAISS) → 500 candidates in ~10 ms. Ranking: gradient boosting on user×item×context features → top 20 in ~30 ms. The comparison baseline: bestsellers.

5. PIPELINE: nightly embedding recompute; the ranker retrains weekly; features in a feature store, the real-time part (recent views) in Redis.

6. SERVING: a precomputed ANN + CPU ranker + business rules (filter purchased, diversify categories). Fallback: the cached top — the feed never goes down.

7. ROLLOUT/MONITORING: an offline gate → A/B 5% → 50% → 100%; monitor CTR, latency, score drift; rollback via a feature flag.

Expected interviewer probes: why not an end-to-end network (latency/cost); what if CTR grows but revenue falls (trust the North star, add margin to the ranking target); the biggest risk (training/serving skew — cured by the feature store and logging actual inference features).

system_design.q: Design an anti-fraud system for a payment service (400K transactions/day, ~0.1% fraud, a decision in 200 ms).
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: minimize fraud losses subject to a false-block constraint; 200 ms synchronously in the payment flow; the manual review team — 500 cases/day.

2. METRICS: money: caught fraud ($) − false-block cost ($) − operating cost. Offline: PR-AUC, recall at the 500-alerts/day budget. Guardrails: payment conversion, block complaint share.

3. DATA: transactions + profile + device + behavioral aggregates over windows (1h/24h/30d), strictly point-in-time. Labels lag (chargebacks up to 90 days) — account for label delay in validation. 1:1000 imbalance.

4. MODEL: a three-tier system: (a) hard rules (sanction lists, limits) — instant; (b) boosting with class weights — the main scorer; (c) a manual review queue for the gray zone. Thresholds: block / step-up verification (3DS) / pass — from the cost matrix. Probability calibration to prioritize by expected damage P(fraud)·amount.

5. VALIDATION: strictly by time; separately — robustness to adversarial behavior (fraudsters adapt → fast retraining cycles, fresh behavioral features).

6. SERVING: real-time features from Redis/stream; p99 < 100 ms for scoring; fallback — rules only (tier a): the system never blocks payments because ML is down.

7. MONITORING: hourly score drift (fraud attacks are sudden!), alert share, delayed quality from chargebacks. The domain's specialty: concept drift here is hostile and permanent — retrain weekly or on alert.

Expected probes: why not block everything suspicious (conversion is dearer); how 90-day label lag affects retraining (train on data ≥ 90 days old + proxy labels); the feedback loop (blocked transactions never get a true label — keep a share of 'passed under observation').

system_design.q: Design a demand forecasting system for a retail chain (2000 stores, 30K SKUs, weekly ordering).
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: reduce write-offs and out-of-stock; horizons: week (orders), month (logistics), year (contracts); a batch forecast by Sunday night — latency doesn't matter, deadline reliability does.

2. METRICS: business: $ write-offs + estimated out-of-stock losses, availability SLA ≥ 95%. Offline: WAPE by segment, bias (over- and under-forecast separately!), quantile loss. Consistency: the forecast must not jump between runs.

3. DATA: sales (corrupted by out-of-stock! zero sales ≠ zero demand — recover censored demand), the promo calendar, prices, weather, holidays. The hierarchy: country → region → store → SKU.

4. MODEL: the baseline ladder: seasonal naive → moving average → one LightGBM across all series with lags/windows/calendar (the industry standard, M5). Quantile forecasts (P50 for planning, P90 for perishables' safety stock). New-SKU cold start via category analogues.

5. VALIDATION: rolling by time, 6 windows; nested for tuning; adversarial validation of train vs recent months (covid periods!).

6. INTEGRATION: the API returns not a number but a recommended order with an interval; buyers can override — logged and analyzed (rising overrides = falling trust). Fallback: previous version → seasonal naive → manual ordering.

7. MONITORING: source freshness before the pipeline runs; output drift; delayed WAPE as actuals arrive (labels come fast — convenient!); the aging test → weekly retraining cadence.

Expected probes: why one model for 60M series instead of 60M models (cross-series generalization, cold start, maintenance); promo cannibalization (neighbor-SKU promo features); the A/B unit is the store; switchback if stores are few.

system_design.q: Design e-commerce catalog search with ML ranking.
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: search conversion (search → purchase); 100 ms per query; 10M queries/day; a long tail of typos and conversational phrasing.

2. METRICS: North star: conversion of search sessions, revenue per search session. Offline: NDCG@10 on clicks/purchases with position-bias correction. Guardrails: zero-results rate, latency.

3. DATA: query→impression→click→purchase logs (clicks are position-biased — debias with click models or weighting). Assessor labels to calibrate top queries.

4. ARCHITECTURE: classic multi-stage: (a) typo correction + normalization; (b) retrieval: BM25 (lexical) + embedding search (semantic, bi-encoder + ANN) — the hybrid covers both exact SKUs and 'something warm for autumn'; (c) ranking: LambdaMART/boosting on relevance features (text scores, popularity, margin, personal); (d) the business layer: stock availability, promo boosts.

5. PIPELINE: incremental catalog indexing; weekly ranker retraining on fresh clicks; the typo dictionary mined from 'query → self-corrected' logs.

6. SERVING: an inverted index + an in-memory ANN index; a hot-query cache (Zipf: the top-1000 queries = 30% of traffic). Fallback: pure BM25 without the ML ranker.

7. EXPERIMENTS: interleaving for fast ranker comparisons (more sensitive than classic A/B), full A/B for decisions; monitoring: zero-results, query distribution drift, CTR@1.

Expected probes: fighting position bias (click models, randomizing the top-2 slots on a small share); a new product's cold start in results (content scores without behavior); why not rank by clicks alone (clickbait items; optimize purchase with margin).

system_design.q: Design an automatic content moderation system (photos + listing text, 1M new listings a day).
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: block prohibited content (weapons, counterfeits, scams) before publication; a 2-minute publication SLA; 20 moderators (≈ 40K reviews/day max); error costs are asymmetric by category (missing weapons ≫ missing spam).

2. METRICS: recall on critical categories at fixed moderator throughput; time to publication; false-block share of honest sellers (guardrail — complaints and churn).

3. DATA: historical moderator decisions (noisy! moderators disagree — measure inter-annotator agreement and account for it), user reports as delayed labels, per-category datasets.

4. MODEL: multimodal scoring: text (fine-tuned BERT), images (CNN/ViT, transfer learning), tabular seller signals (account age, history). Fusion — boosting over the scores. Three-zone logic: auto-block (confidently bad) / moderation queue (gray zone, prioritized by risk × reach) / auto-publish. Thresholds per category (by error cost).

5. VALIDATION: by time; separate test sets per critical category; adversarial tests (blurred photos, obfuscated text — violators adapt).

6. SERVING: an asynchronous pipeline (queue), GPU batching for the networks; the 2-minute SLA allows batching. Fallback: everything to the manual queue prioritized by rules.

7. MONITORING: per-category drift (new scam schemes!), auto-decision share, model-vs-moderator disagreement, queue speed. Active learning: moderators' disputed cases → the next version's labeling.

Expected probes: human-in-the-loop — what to show moderators (max uncertainty × potential damage); measuring recall on the missed (stratified manual audits of published content); label noise (moderator voting on a golden set).

system_design.q: Design a delivery-time (ETA) prediction system for a food delivery service.
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: show the ETA at checkout and update it in flight; errors hit conversion (an inflated ETA) and NPS (an understated one — 'promised 30, delivered in 60'); online, 50 ms; 500K orders/day.

2. METRICS: business: checkout conversion + share of orders 'within the promised window' (more important than mean error!). Offline: MAE and quantile losses; the asymmetry: underestimating is worse than overestimating → forecast the P70–P80 quantile, not the mean. Show a range ('35–45 min') instead of a point.

3. DATA: order stages (confirm → cooking → courier → travel), historical restaurant and courier timings, real-time kitchen load, weather, traffic, geo. Decompose the target: ETA = t_kitchen + t_courier_wait + t_travel — three models beat one end-to-end (and explain better).

4. MODEL: boosting per stage with real-time features (the restaurant's current queue, free couriers in radius); quantile regression. ETA recomputation on events (restaurant accepted, courier picked up) — a cascade of refining models.

5. VALIDATION: by time; separately by segment (new restaurants, rush hour, rain) — worst-case analysis is mandatory: the user remembers one terrible ETA, not the average.

6. SERVING: real-time aggregates in Redis (restaurant load — a 15-min rolling window); p99 < 30 ms; fallback: the restaurant's historical median + a buffer.

7. MONITORING: on-time rate by hour/city; quantile calibration (do 80% of orders really fit in the P80?); sudden drift — weather events, holidays: anomaly alerts on the input streams.

Expected probes: why a quantile, not the mean (cost asymmetry + user psychology); the dispatch feedback loop (the shown ETA affects courier assignment and thus the timing itself — only end-to-end experiments); a new restaurant's cold start (cuisine-category priors + fast transition to its own stats).

system_design.q: Design a credit scoring system for a neobank with regulatory requirements.
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: an application decision < 1 minute; explainability of every rejection (regulation!); no discrimination on protected attributes; the default label matures 6–12 months.

2. METRICS: business: expected portfolio profit = approved volume × margin − expected losses (PD × EAD × LGD). ML: ROC-AUC/Gini on an out-of-time test, PD calibration (probabilities must be honest frequencies — the basis of risk pricing). Stability: the scorecard's monthly PSI.

3. DATA: the application, credit bureaus, transactions (for existing customers), behavioral. Strictly point-in-time. The reject-inference trap: we train only on approvals (rejects have no label) → sample bias; methods: partially approving a random slice of the gray zone, extrapolation.

4. MODEL: two tracks: (a) an interpretable scorecard (logreg on WoE binning) — the regulatory production track, every point explainable; (b) boosting as the challenger — measuring what interpretability 'costs' in accuracy. SHAP for rejection explanations. A fairness audit: metrics by protected group, proxy features (neighborhood index ≈ ethnicity) — detect and exclude.

5. VALIDATION: out-of-time (not just out-of-sample!); vintage analysis — quality by issuance cohort; stress scenarios (a downturn).

6. SERVING: a synchronous API with the bureaus; a full audit log of every decision (input, model version, score, decision) — a regulatory requirement; no silent model updates — every version passes a validation committee.

7. MONITORING: default vintages vs predicted PD (calibration in battle); input and score PSI; approval rate by segment (a sudden shift = a data problem or population drift).

Expected probes: why not boosting alone (regulation + the cost of explanation errors); reject inference in depth; updating the model when the label matures a year (early-delinquency 30+/60+ proxy labels).

system_design.q: Design an LLM-based automatic support answering system with quality control (RAG).
system_design.a:
1. REQUIREMENTS: automate 40–60% of 5000 tickets/day without a CSAT drop; invented answers about money/terms are unacceptable; latency < 5 s; inference cost within budget.

2. METRICS: North star: share of tickets closed without a human at CSAT ≥ the human level. Proxies: escalation share, repeat contacts on the same issue (the main 'the answer didn't help' marker). Generation quality: hallucination share on an audit sample, groundedness (the answer is supported by retrieved documents).

3. DATA/KNOWLEDGE: the knowledge base (FAQ, policies, order statuses via API) — chunking, embeddings, versioning: a stale policy = a confidently wrong answer. Ticket history with resolutions for few-shot examples.

4. ARCHITECTURE: RAG: an intent classifier (routing: transactional questions → API calls, knowledge questions → the retriever) → hybrid search (BM25 + embeddings) → an LLM with a 'answer only from the context' system prompt → answer validators: a groundedness checker (a second model verifies source support), a forbidden-promise filter (compensations, legal obligations), an uncertainty detector → a confident answer to the customer / a draft for the agent / direct escalation. Three zones, like in moderation.

5. EVALUATION: a golden set of 500 tickets with references; LLM-as-judge to scale evaluation + a weekly manual audit of 100 random ones; prompt regression tests on every change.

6. SERVING: a frequent-question cache; a small model for intent, the big one only for generation; streaming responses. Fallback: on any doubt — a human (the error costs are asymmetric: silently handing to an agent is cheap; lying to a customer is expensive).

7. MONITORING: auto-close, escalation and repeat-contact shares by day; topic drift (a new product → new questions → a knowledge-base gap); cost per ticket.

Expected probes: measuring hallucinations in production (groundedness scoring + repeat contacts as a proxy); why RAG instead of fine-tuning (knowledge changes daily; RAG updates without retraining); safety (prompt injection inside ticket text — instruction isolation, output validation).
